text:
# Problem 033: Post-Norm Residual (RMSNorm + Residual Add)

## Op graph

```
[sublayer_output: bf16, B×S×4096]   [residual: bf16, B×S×4096]   [weight: bf16, 4096]
        │                                    │                        │
        ▼                                    │                        │
  ┌─[load bf16 tile]─┐                       │                        │
  │                  │                       │                        │
  │  [to fp32]       │                       │                        │
  │  [square: x²]    │                       │                        │
  │  [reduce_mean    │                       │                        │
  │   along H=4096]  │                       │                        │
  │  [+ eps]         │                       │                        │
  │  [rsqrt]         │                       │                        │
  │  [broadcast      │                       │                        │
  │   mul: x·rsqrt]  │                       │                        │
  │  [broadcast      │◄──[weight, fp32]──────┘                        │
  │   mul: ·weight]  │                                                │
  │  [to bf16]       │                                                │
  │                  │                                                │
  └────────┬─────────┘                                                │
           │                                                          │
           ▼                                                          │
     [add: + residual] ◄──────────────[load bf16 tile]────────────────┘
           │
           ▼
   [store bf16 output: B×S×4096]
```

**Fused kernel** (all ops in one launch): 3 bf16 reads, 1 bf16 write.
**Reference** (PyTorch eager): ~7 intermediate kernels, each reading/writing
fp32 intermediates to GMEM.

## Per-shape roofline

All shapes are **memory-bound** — arithmetic intensity is far below the
BF16-TC ridge (292 FLOP/B) and even the FP32-vector ridge (9.74 FLOP/B).

### Arithmetic intensity (fused kernel)

- FLOPs (fp32): square(N) + reduce_mean(N) + rsqrt(B·S) + normalize_mul(N) +
  scale_mul(N) + residual_add(N) ≈ **5N** flops
- Bytes (fused): 2× bf16 read (sublayer + residual) + 1× bf16 read (weight) +
  1× bf16 write (output) = **6N + 8 KB** bytes
- AI = 5N / 6N = **0.833 FLOP/byte**

FP32 ridge: 75 TF / 7.7 TB/s = 9.74 FLOP/B → **margin: 11.7× below ridge**.
BF16-TC ridge: 2250 TF / 7.7 TB/s = 292 FLOP/B → **margin: 350× below ridge**.

All computation is "free" — every shape is limited by HBM bandwidth.

### Per-shape breakdown

| # | B | S | N = B·S·4096 | HBM traffic (fused) | T_SOL at 100% BW | T_ach at 85% BW | Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 16 | 1024 | 67.1M | 403 MB | 52.3 µs | 61.5 µs | **large** |
| 1 | 8 | 2048 | 67.1M | 403 MB | 52.3 µs | 61.5 µs | **large** |
| 2 | 32 | 256 | 33.6M | 201 MB | 26.1 µs | 30.7 µs | **large** |
| 3 | 8 | 997 | 32.7M | 196 MB | 25.5 µs | 30.0 µs | **large** |
| 4 | 16 | 512 | 33.6M | 201 MB | 26.1 µs | 30.7 µs | **large** |
| 5 | 4 | 2048 | 33.6M | 201 MB | 26.1 µs | 30.7 µs | **large** |
| 8 | 2 | 4096 | 33.6M | 201 MB | 26.1 µs | 30.7 µs | **large** |
| 7 | 2 | 2053 | 16.8M | 101 MB | 13.1 µs | 15.4 µs | **medium** |
| 9 | 8 | 512 | 16.8M | 101 MB | 13.1 µs | 15.4 µs | **medium** |
| 13 | 2 | 2048 | 16.8M | 101 MB | 13.1 µs | 15.4 µs | **medium** |
| 14 | 8 | 256 | 8.4M | 50 MB | 6.5 µs | 7.7 µs | **small** |
| 11 | 1 | 1024 | 4.2M | 25 MB | 3.3 µs | 3.8 µs | **small** |
| 12 | 2 | 293 | 2.4M | 14.4 MB | 1.9 µs | 2.2 µs | **small** |
| 10 | 4 | 128 | 2.1M | 12.6 MB | 1.6 µs | 1.9 µs | **small** |
| 6 | 1 | 131 | 537K | 3.2 MB | 0.42 µs | 0.49 µs | **small** |
| 15 | 1 | 128 | 524K | 3.1 MB | 0.41 µs | 0.48 µs | **small** |

**Regime definitions:**
- **large** (≥30 µs at 85% BW): memory-bandwidth-bound, launch overhead
  negligible (<1% of runtime). Optimize for HBM bandwidth %.
- **medium** (10–30 µs): still memory-bound, but launch overhead (~5–10 µs)
  becomes a measurable fraction.
- **small** (<10 µs): launch/latency-bound. The theoretical HBM time is below
  the kernel-launch floor (~3–10 µs per launch). CUDA graphs or persistent
  kernels are mandatory to reach the roofline.

### Reference vs fused traffic

For the largest shape (B=16, S=1024, N=67.1M):

| Variant | HBM reads | HBM writes | Total traffic | Fused ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reference (7 kernels) | ~1.47 GB | ~1.07 GB | ~2.55 GB | 1× |
| Fused (1 kernel) | 0.27 GB | 0.13 GB | 0.40 GB | **6.3× less** |

The reference materializes fp32 intermediates (268 MB each) to GMEM between
every op. Fusion eliminates all of that — the theoretical speedup ceiling from
traffic reduction alone is ~6.3×.

## Ranked approaches

### Approach 1 (rank 1): Triton fused kernel with autotuning

**What:** Single Triton kernel that loads bf16 tiles, computes RMSNorm +
residual in fp32, stores bf16 output. One `tl.program` with `autotune` over
block sizes.

**Why rank 1:** This is the textbook fusion pattern for a memory-bound
elementwise+reduction chain. Triton handles bf16 loads/stores, vectorization,
and occupancy natively. Expected to reach 75–85% of HBM bandwidth on large
shapes, which is the terminal ceiling for this op class (see kb: stop rule —
"lower DSLs gain nothing" for memory-bound Triton kernels near terminal BW).

**Key design decisions:**
- **Reduction dimension = H=4096.** Tile across H with `BLOCK_H` (e.g., 256 or
  512). Each program instance handles one `(batch, seq)` element, accumulating
  the mean-of-squares over H tiles in fp32.
- **Parallelize over B·S.** Grid = `(B·S, 1, 1)` or a 2D grid
  `(cdiv(B·S, BLOCK_BS), 1, 1)` with a loop over B·S tiles within each block.
- **Two-pass or one-pass?** Two-pass: (1) compute variance, (2) normalize +
  scale + add residual. The reduction result (a scalar per B·S element) is so
  tiny that writing it to GMEM and re-reading costs ~nothing. But a one-pass
  design with blocking along H avoids the second pass: load H tiles, accumulate
  sum-of-squares, then in the same kernel loop normalize + scale + add.
  **Recommend one-pass with tile-level blocking** — the H dimension is 4096,
  which is perfect for a few tiles of BLOCK_H=512 or 1024.
- **Autotune space:** `BLOCK_H ∈ {256, 512, 1024}`, `num_warps ∈ {4, 8, 16}`,
  `num_stages ∈ {1, 2, 3}`. Shape-specialize: each of the 16 shapes gets its
  own best config.
- **Precision:** All computation in fp32 (Triton `float32`). The reference
  does fp32, and the tight atol=1e-5 on small shapes requires it. bf16
  accumulation in the reduction would diverge after summing 4096 elements.

**Expected outcome:** ~2–4× over reference on large shapes (dominated by
traffic reduction), ~3–6× on small shapes (traffic reduction + launch
elimination). Should reach 0.75–0.85× SOL.

### Approach 2 (rank 2): CUDA-graph the fused kernel for small shapes

**What:** Wrap the fused Triton kernel in a CUDA graph for shapes where the
kernel runtime is <10 µs. The eval loop calls the kernel 50 times (timed) +
10 warmup; a graph captures the launch and amortizes the CPU-side overhead.

**Why rank 2:** The small shapes (#6, #10, #11, #12, #14, #15) have
theoretical HBM times of 0.4–7.7 µs. At those sizes, CPU launch overhead
(~3–10 µs/kernel) can be 2–20× the GPU work. CUDA graphs eliminate the
per-iteration launch gap. This is a standard ladder-C technique
(kb/optimization-recipe.md §Ladder C: "CUDA graphs / reduce-overhead …
1.81× e2e").

**Key design decisions:**
- **Graph at the `run()` level, not the kernel level.** The eval loop calls
  `run(sublayer_output, residual, weight, eps)` → returns `output`. Capture
  the entire call in a graph, with the input tensors as graph parameters so
  they can be updated between replays.
- **Threshold:** Apply only to shapes where `T_ach < 15 µs` (medium + small
  regimes: shapes #6, #7, #9, #10, #11, #12, #13, #14, #15). For large shapes
  the kernel work dominates and the graph's launch savings are negligible.
- **Cold-L2 compatibility:** The grader flushes L2 before _every_ iteration.
  The graph captures the kernel, not the flush — the flush happens between
  graph replays, so the kernel still sees cold L2 each time. This is correct
  and doesn't break the scoring model.
- **Alternative: `torch.compile(mode='reduce-overhead')`.** Eager-mode
  `torch.compile` with `reduce-overhead` uses CUDA graphs internally. Apply
  this to the entire fused kernel function. Simpler than hand-authoring graphs
  but less control over which shapes get graph'd.

**Expected outcome:** Recovers the last 10–30% on small shapes by eliminating
launch overhead. On large shapes, no change (kernel work dominates).

### Approach 3 (rank 3): Persistent grid-stride kernel for small shapes

**What:** A single persistent kernel where the grid is fixed at 148 CTAs (one
per SM), and each CTA loops over B·S work items via a grid-stride loop. This
eliminates both kernel launch overhead AND grid underutilization on small
shapes.

**Why rank 3 (lower priority):** This is a higher-effort version of the CUDA
graph approach. A persistent kernel helps when the natural grid size (B·S)
is smaller than 148 SMs — e.g., shape #15 (B=1, S=128) has only 128 work
items, so a naive grid of 128 blocks leaves 20 SMs idle. A persistent kernel
with 148 blocks would each handle ~1 work item, wasting SMs on tiny tiles.
The real fix for small shapes is the CUDA graph (approach 2), not persistent
kernels. Reserve this for if approach 2 doesn't close the gap.

**Key design decisions:**
- Grid = 148 (one per SM). Each CTA atomically claims a `B·S` index and
  processes it.
- Only worth it for shapes where `B·S < 148` (shapes #6, #10, #11, #12, #15)
  — those are the ones where a naive grid leaves SMs unused.
- Same fused computation as approach 1, just with a persistent scheduler.

**Expected outcome:** Marginal (1–5%) over approach 2 on the smallest shapes.
Not worth the complexity unless approach 2 leaves a gap.

## Summary

| Shape regime | Count | Primary lever | Secondary lever |
|---|---|---|---|
| **large** (B·S·4096 ≥ 33.6M) | 7 shapes | Fusion (approach 1) | Tile-size autotuning |
| **medium** (8.4M–16.8M) | 4 shapes | Fusion (approach 1) | CUDA graph (approach 2) |
| **small** (<8.4M) | 5 shapes | CUDA graph (approach 2) | Persistent kernel (approach 3) |

**Fusion is the dominant lever across all shapes** — reducing GMEM traffic
6.3× vs the reference. The small shapes additionally need launch-overhead
elimination. The medium shapes are a transition zone where both matter.

**Stop condition:** Achieved bandwidth ≥ 85% of HBM peak (~6.5 TB/s) on
large shapes. At 0.833 FLOP/byte, this kernel can never become compute-bound;
there is no tensor-core or lower-DSL play that changes the physics. Once
bandwidth is near the STREAM ceiling, move on.
